title: The Concept of Momentum
html:
Speed, not position

Most of the tools we have met so far describe where price is: above a moving average, near support, inside a channel. Momentum oscillators describe something different and arguably more interesting — how fast price is moving and whether that speed is increasing or decreasing. A rally that is accelerating is healthy. A rally that is still rising but slowing down is, in physics terms, decelerating; the buyers are getting tired even though they have not yet given up. Momentum is the study of that deceleration before it shows up as a turn in price.

Definition.
Momentum is the rate of change of price over a fixed lookback. The simplest form is Rate of Change: ROCn = (Ptoday − Pn bars ago) ÷ Pn bars ago × 100. An oscillator is any indicator that fluctuates around a centerline (or within a bounded 0–100 range), rising when momentum builds and falling when it fades.

A worked rate-of-change

Suppose ACME closes at 100, then 102, 105, 109, 112, 113.5, 114. Using a 1-bar ROC we measure the day-over-day push:

1-bar Rate of Change
Close → ROC vs prior bar 100 → 102 ROC = +2.00% accelerating 102 → 105 ROC = +2.94% accelerating 105 → 109 ROC = +3.81% peak thrust 109 → 112 ROC = +2.75% slowing 112 → 113.5 ROC = +1.34% slowing 113.5 → 114 ROC = +0.44% nearly flat

Notice the crucial point: price keeps making new highs the entire way (100 → 114), yet momentum peaked at bar 3 and rolled over while price was still climbing. The ROC line traces a hill that crests ahead of price. This is the single most valuable property of momentum: it is a leading characteristic of a trend's internal energy, not a lagging one like a moving average.

In the chart above, follow the price candles into a steep, accelerating run. Now drop your eyes to the lower pane. The oscillator climbs hard during the meat of the move, but as price goes vertical the oscillator stops making new highs and begins to flatten. That flattening is momentum telling you the marginal buyer is paying more for less follow-through. The rally is not over — but its fuel gauge is dropping.

Why we bound the oscillator

Raw ROC is unbounded: a violent move can read +30% one day and the scale becomes useless across different instruments and eras. The great innovation of oscillators like RSI and Stochastics is normalisation — squashing momentum into a fixed 0–100 band so that "extreme" means the same thing on a $5 stock and a $5,000 index, in 1998 and today. That lets us draw horizontal reference lines (70/30, 80/20) and speak a common language about overbought and oversold.

Insight.
Momentum measures the derivative of price. Just as a car can still be moving forward while braking, a market can still be rising while its momentum collapses. Oscillators are built to make that braking visible.

What oscillators are good and bad at

  • Good at: flagging exhaustion, timing entries inside ranges, and revealing divergence between price and internal energy.
  • Bad at: telling you direction in a strong trend — they will scream "overbought" for weeks while a bull market grinds higher (Lesson 8.3).

Keep that asymmetry in mind through this whole part. An oscillator is a tachometer, not a steering wheel. It tells you the engine's RPM; it does not tell you which way the road bends. Used as a tachometer it is superb. Used as a steering wheel it will drive you off a cliff — which is exactly the trap we will dismantle in Lesson 8.6.

▸Try It

Goal: feel how momentum leads price.

  1. Take any 10 consecutive daily closes from a chart you follow.
  2. Compute the 1-bar ROC for each day: (today − yesterday) ÷ yesterday × 100.
  3. Mark the bar where ROC peaked, and the bar where price peaked.
  4. Confirm the ROC peak came first (or at the same time). Note how many bars of warning it gave.

title: RSI: Calculation and Overbought/Oversold
html:
What RSI actually computes

The Relative Strength Index, designed by J. Welles Wilder in 1978, answers one question: over the last N bars, how much of the total movement was up versus down? It is not "relative strength" in the sense of one stock versus another — that is a different concept. RSI compares a market's gains to its own losses.

Formula.
RS = (Average Gain over N) ÷ (Average Loss over N). Then RSI = 100 − 100 ÷ (1 + RS). The default is N = 14. If average loss = 0, RSI = 100; if average gain = 0, RSI = 0. The output is bounded in [0, 100].

Building a 14-period RSI by hand

The first calculation uses a simple average of the up-moves and down-moves over the first 14 bars. Suppose over 14 days NOVA's gains and losses sum as follows:

RSI step-by-step (N = 14)
Sum of up-closes over 14 bars = 10.50 Sum of down-closes over 14 bars = 3.00 Average Gain = 10.50 ÷ 14 = 0.750 Average Loss = 3.00 ÷ 14 = 0.214 RS = 0.750 ÷ 0.214 = 3.50 RSI = 100 − 100 ÷ (1 + 3.50) = 77.8 → deep into overbought

After the first bar, Wilder uses a smoothed (Wilder) average so the line does not jump: Avg Gain = (prior Avg Gain × 13 + current Gain) ÷ 14, and likewise for losses. This smoothing is why RSI behaves like an exponential moving average of relative strength rather than a jumpy raw ratio.

Insight.
RSI is just a ratio of average up-moves to average down-moves, re-scaled. RSI = 50 means up and down energy were balanced over the window. RSI = 80 means up-moves outweighed down-moves roughly 4-to-1. That intuition is more useful than memorising the formula.

Reading the 70/30 zones

Wilder's convention: above 70 is overbought, below 30 is oversold. These are not buy/sell buttons — they are statements about recent kinetic energy. An RSI of 78 says "the move up has been lopsided and fast; it is statistically stretched." That is all. Whether to fade it depends entirely on context, which is the theme of the rest of this part.

Study the chart. NOVA is chopping sideways. Each time price pokes the top of the range the RSI pane reads near 78; each time it flushes to the bottom the RSI reads near 24. In this range environment the 70/30 lines are gold — they front-run the reversals because there is no trend to overpower the mean-reversion instinct of buyers and sellers. Mark how the RSI extremes coincide almost exactly with the price extremes.

The skepticism you must carry

The reading "78" carries zero information about whether price will turn. In the range above it did. In the trend of the next lesson it will not — price will rip higher with RSI pinned over 70 for weeks. The number is identical; the meaning is opposite. So treat 70/30 as a condition flag, never a signal. A condition (stretched) plus a trigger (a reversal bar, a divergence, a break of a micro-level) is a setup. A condition alone is a coin flip.

Pitfall.
"RSI over 70, so I'll short" is the most expensive sentence in technical analysis. In a strong uptrend RSI living above 70 is a sign of strength, not a sell. Selling overbought into an uptrend is how new traders donate capital. We dissect exactly why in 8.3.

Choosing N

Lower N (say 7) makes RSI faster and noisier, hitting extremes more often — useful for short-term swing work but more false flags. Higher N (21) is slower and smoother, with fewer but more meaningful extremes. The 14 default is a reasonable middle. Whatever you pick, keep it consistent so your sense of "normal" for that setting stays calibrated.

▸Try It

Goal: build RSI intuition from raw numbers.

  1. Pull 15 daily closes for any instrument.
  2. Classify each day's change as a gain or a loss, and sum each over the 14 changes.
  3. Compute Average Gain, Average Loss, RS, and RSI using the formula above.
  4. Compare your hand figure to the platform's RSI(14) reading — they should be within rounding once a few bars of smoothing pass.

title: RSI Divergence and Why Overbought Can Stay Overbought
html:
Why overbought stays overbought

This lesson resolves the paradox that ruins beginners. In a powerful uptrend, RSI can sit above 70 for days or weeks and price keeps climbing the whole time. The reason is mechanical. RSI = 100 − 100 ÷ (1 + RS). For RSI to fall, the average loss must grow relative to the average gain. But in a strong trend, pullbacks are tiny and brief — average loss stays small, average gain stays large, RS stays huge, and RSI stays pinned high. The oscillator is not malfunctioning; it is correctly reporting that down-moves are scarce.

Definition.
An overbought trend is one where RSI repeatedly holds above 70 (or 80) on shallow pullbacks. This is a hallmark of strength. Some traders shift the zones in a confirmed uptrend to 80/40 — treating 40 as the buy-the-dip floor rather than 30.

Look at APEX. The trend marches up the 20-day average. In the lower pane RSI vaults above 70 early and simply refuses to come back — every dip in price barely dents it. Anyone who shorted the first 70 print was run over for the next three weeks. The lesson is visceral: in a trend, overbought is the cost of admission to being long, not a reason to sell.

What actually marks the top: divergence

If the 70 line does not call the top, what does? Often, divergence. Divergence is a disagreement between price and momentum: price makes a higher high, but RSI makes a lower high. Price is still climbing; its internal energy is not. The new high was made on weaker thrust — exactly the deceleration we measured in Lesson 8.1, now visible on a bounded scale.

Bearish divergence at the APEX top
Price high #1 = 118.0 RSI = 79 Price high #2 = 122.0 RSI = 71 (higher price, LOWER RSI) Verdict: price ↑ but momentum ↓ → bearish divergence Trigger: only act when price then breaks the swing-low / the 20-SMA

The two flavours mirror each other:

  • Bearish (regular) divergence: price higher high, RSI lower high — warns of a top. Best near or above 70.
  • Bullish (regular) divergence: price lower low, RSI higher low — warns of a bottom. Best near or below 30.

This second chart isolates the classic. Price grinds to a final, slightly higher peak; the RSI pane visibly tops at a lower level than its previous peak. The dotted disagreement between the two slopes is the divergence. Then price breaks its recent swing low — that break is the trigger. Notice the order of operations: divergence is the condition; the break is the confirmation. Acting on divergence alone is how you get chopped up, because divergence can persist as price grinds higher in a parabola.

Pitfall.
Divergence is not a timing tool by itself — it is a warning that can repeat several times before the turn. The strongest trends print three or four bearish divergences before they actually break. Never short purely on the divergence; wait for price to confirm by breaking structure.
Insight.
Hidden divergence is the trend-continuation cousin: in an uptrend, price makes a higher low while RSI makes a lower low — signalling the dip is bought and the trend resumes. Regular divergence warns of reversal; hidden divergence confirms continuation.

Putting it together

The mature read of RSI in a trend is therefore two-layered. First, accept that overbought is normal and stop fading it. Second, watch for the day the new price high comes with weaker RSI — that is your early warning — and then demand a price-structure break before you act. That sequence (overbought tolerated → divergence noticed → structure break confirmed) turns RSI from a beginner's trap into a genuine edge.

▸Try It

Goal: separate condition from trigger.

  1. Find a past uptrend that topped out. Mark every bar where RSI first crossed 70.
  2. Count how many of those 70-crosses were followed by an immediate reversal (usually almost none).
  3. Now find the final two price highs. Record the RSI at each — confirm the second high had lower RSI (bearish divergence).
  4. Mark where price then broke its prior swing low. That break, not the divergence, was the actionable moment.

title: Stochastics: %K and %D
html:
A different question

George Lane's Stochastic Oscillator asks: where does today's close sit within the recent high-low range? The premise is behavioural — in an uptrend closes tend to cluster near the top of the range; in a downtrend they cluster near the bottom. When closes start finishing low even though price is high, the move is weakening. Where RSI compares gains to losses, Stochastics compares the close to its range.

Formula.
%K = (Close − Lowest LowN) ÷ (Highest HighN − Lowest LowN) × 100. Then %D = the 3-period simple moving average of %K. The popular "14,3,3" setting means N=14, an initial 3-period smoothing of raw %K (slow stochastic), and a 3-period %D. Output is bounded 0–100; overbought above 80, oversold below 20.

A worked %K

Stochastic %K (N = 14)
Highest high over 14 bars = 110.0 Lowest low over 14 bars = 100.0 Today's close = 108.0 %K = (108 − 100) ÷ (110 − 100) × 100 = 80.0 → closing high in range If close were 101 → %K = (1 ÷ 10) × 100 = 10.0 → closing low in range

So a close of 108 inside a 100–110 range scores 80: buyers controlled the finish. A close of 101 in the same range scores 10: sellers controlled the finish even though the high was 110. That is the elegance — it measures who won the day relative to the battlefield.

The %K / %D crossover

The signal traders watch is the crossover of the fast %K through the slower %D, especially when it happens in an extreme zone. A %K crossing up through %D from below 20 is a classic range-bottom buy trigger. A %K crossing down through %D from above 80 is a range-top sell trigger. The smoothing of the slow stochastic deliberately cuts down the whipsaw that the raw (fast) version produces.

In ORION's range, walk through the lower pane. At the bottom of the channel %K plunges below 20, then hooks up and crosses above %D — that hook-and-cross is the entry cue, and it lands right as price bounces off support. At the top of the range the mirror happens: %K climbs above 80, then rolls down through %D as price stalls at resistance. In a clean range these crossovers are remarkably well-timed because the oscillator is built for exactly this mean-reverting behaviour.

Insight.
Stochastics is the most range-native of the common oscillators. It fires frequently and reaches extremes easily, which makes it superb inside a defined range and treacherous in a trend — it will give crossover buy signals all the way down a waterfall.

RSI vs Stochastics — a quick contrast

AspectRSIStochastics
MeasuresAvg gain vs avg lossClose vs recent range
Extreme lines70 / 3080 / 20
SpeedSmoother, fewer signalsFaster, more signals
Primary signalDivergence, zone exit%K/%D crossover in zone
Best habitatTrend (divergence) & rangeRange / chop

Many traders run both: RSI for the divergence read and trend strength, Stochastics for the crisp entry trigger inside a range. Just remember they often disagree, and disagreement is information — not an error to be averaged away.

Pitfall.
Because Stochastics maxes out so easily, a single explosive up-day can drive %K to 95+ and keep it there. Selling that first 80-cross in a breakout is a classic way to short strength. Confirm you are in a range, not a breakout, before trading the crossover.
▸Try It

Goal: read crossovers in their proper habitat.

  1. Add a 14,3,3 slow stochastic to a chart currently in a sideways range.
  2. Mark each %K/%D crossover that occurred below 20 or above 80.
  3. Note how each lined up with a touch of range support or resistance.
  4. Now switch to a chart in a strong downtrend and watch the same crossovers fire repeatedly on the way down — proof that habitat is everything.

title: CCI and Williams %R
html:
Two more lenses on the same idea

RSI and Stochastics are not the only momentum gauges. The Commodity Channel Index (Donald Lambert, 1980) and Williams %R (Larry Williams) measure stretch and range-position with different scales and quirks. Knowing them broadens your toolkit and, more importantly, teaches you that "extreme" is a relative idea each indicator defines for itself.

CCI: deviation from the typical price

Formula.
CCI = (Typical Price − SMA of TP) ÷ (0.015 × Mean Deviation), where Typical Price = (High + Low + Close) ÷ 3. The 0.015 constant is chosen so that roughly 70–80% of values fall within ±100. Unlike RSI, CCI is unbounded — it can read +250 or −300 in violent moves.
CCI worked example
Typical Price today = (114 + 110 + 113) ÷ 3 = 112.33 20-bar SMA of TP = 106.00 Mean deviation = 3.20 CCI = (112.33 − 106.00) ÷ (0.015 × 3.20) = 6.33 ÷ 0.048 = +131.9 → thrust above +100

The standard read: above +100 indicates a strong up-thrust (often the start of a trend leg, not its end); below −100 a strong down-thrust. Because it is unbounded, CCI is excellent at flagging the initiation of a powerful move — a cross above +100 can mark a breakout's ignition rather than an overbought top.

Picture CCI on VERTEX's breakout. As price clears the prior ceiling, CCI spikes through +100. A naive oscillator trader sees "overbought" and fades it — and gets steamrolled, because for CCI the +100 cross during a breakout is a go signal. The volatility expansion you can see in the lower pane confirms real participation behind the thrust. This is the opposite instinct from Stochastics, and that contrast is the whole point of learning multiple tools.

Williams %R: an inverted stochastic

Formula.
%R = (Highest HighN − Close) ÷ (Highest HighN − Lowest LowN) × −100. The scale runs from 0 (close at the very top of the range) to −100 (close at the very bottom). Overbought is roughly −20 to 0; oversold is −80 to −100.

Williams %R is essentially the Stochastic %K flipped onto a negative scale. A reading near −10 means the close is sitting near the top of the recent range — strong, and in a powerful uptrend it can stay pinned there for a long stretch, just like RSI staying above 70.

Williams %R worked example
Highest high (14) = 115.0 Lowest low (14) = 100.0 Close = 113.5 %R = (115 − 113.5) ÷ (115 − 100) × −100 = (1.5 ÷ 15) × −100 = −10.0 → pinned near the top, strong

On VERTEX's advance, %R hugging −10 day after day is the signature of a healthy trend. Reading that as "sell, it's overbought" is the same error we keep warning about. The correct read is that closes keep finishing at the top of the range — buyers are in firm control.

Insight.
Use CCI to catch the ignition of a move (cross of ±100) and Williams %R to gauge whether closes are still finishing strong within the range. Both, like every oscillator, flip meaning between trend and range — the subject of the next lesson.
Pitfall.
CCI's unbounded scale tempts traders to fade ever-bigger numbers ("it's at +200, surely it turns now"). In a true trend CCI can ride above +100 for the entire leg. Treat ±100 as a momentum-state line, not a reversal line.
▸Try It

Goal: compare the personalities of CCI and Williams %R.

  1. Add both CCI(20) and Williams %R(14) to the same chart.
  2. On a recent breakout, note whether CCI crossing +100 marked ignition or exhaustion.
  3. On a strong trend, watch how long %R stays pinned past −20 (or −80 in a downtrend).
  4. Write one sentence on how each behaves differently from RSI in the same spots.

title: Oscillators in a Trend vs a Range
html:
The single most important oscillator skill

If you remember one idea from this entire part, make it this: an oscillator signal means the opposite thing in a trend versus a range. The identical RSI-30 print is a beautiful buy in a sideways market and a falling-knife disaster in a downtrend. The number does not change; the environment changes its meaning. Mastering oscillators is really mastering the question "what regime am I in?" before you read the indicator.

Definition.
In a range, use oscillators for mean reversion: fade extremes (buy oversold, sell overbought). In a trend, use oscillators for trend confirmation: buy pullbacks toward the midline in the trend direction, ignore the counter-trend extreme, and watch for divergence to warn of the end.

The same RSI-30, two fates

First, the range. HELIX oscillates in a box. When RSI dips to 30 it is sitting at the lower band of the range, sellers are exhausted, and the mean-reversion buy works because there is no larger force pulling price down — the market keeps returning to its average. Fading the extreme is exactly right here.

Now the downtrend — same instrument, same RSI-30 reading. Here price is in a sustained decline. RSI hits 30 and the beginner buys "oversold." But in a downtrend oversold is the normal condition; RSI will read 30 again and again, each time at a lower price. The mean it would revert to is itself falling. The buy that worked in the range becomes a knife-catch that bleeds on every bounce. Same signal, opposite outcome — the only difference is regime.

Pitfall.
The deadliest oscillator mistake is trading mean-reversion signals inside a trend: buying oversold in a downtrend, shorting overbought in an uptrend. This is how oscillators earned their bad reputation — not because the tools are broken, but because they were used in the wrong regime.

How to tell the regime first

Before you trust any oscillator reading, classify the market:

  • Trend tells: price respecting a rising/falling moving average (e.g. 50-SMA), higher highs & higher lows (or the reverse), strong directional ADX, oscillator pinned at one extreme.
  • Range tells: price oscillating between horizontal support and resistance, flat moving averages, low/declining ADX, oscillator swinging cleanly between both extremes.
Regime → how to read the oscillator
RANGE → fade extremes buy 30, sell 70 UPTREND → buy pullbacks buy RSI ~40-50 dips, ignore 70 DOWNTREND → sell rallies short RSI ~50-60 bounces, ignore 30 Any regime → divergence warns of an ending move (needs confirmation)

This last chart shows the trend-correct usage. In HELIX's uptrend the actionable signal is not the overbought tag — it is the pullback, where price retraces to the rising 50-SMA and RSI cools toward 40–50 before turning back up. You are buying a dip in the direction of the trend, using the oscillator to time the entry, not to fight the trend. That is the professional's use of momentum: as a timing aid aligned with the larger force, never against it.

Insight.
A clean shortcut: in an uptrend, raise your "oversold" buy zone to 40 and stop using the 70 sell line entirely; in a downtrend, lower your "overbought" short zone to 60 and stop using the 30 buy line. Let the trend, not the textbook, set the zones.
▸Try It

Goal: make regime classification a reflex.

  1. Open three charts: one clear range, one uptrend, one downtrend.
  2. For each, write the regime FIRST, then add RSI(14).
  3. In the range, mark where fading 70/30 worked.
  4. In the trends, mark where fading the extreme would have lost — and where the in-trend pullback entry would have won instead.

title: How Reliable Oscillator Signals Really Are
html:
The honest answer

How reliable is an oscillator signal? The honest, evidence-based answer is: it depends almost entirely on context, and a naked oscillator signal has little to no edge by itself. Decades of testing converge on the same finding — mechanical "buy RSI below 30, sell above 70" rules are roughly break-even to negative after costs when applied blindly across all markets, because they win in ranges and lose badly in trends, and the losses in trends are larger and longer.

Illustrative backtest: buy RSI < 30, exit at RSI 50
Strategy applied to RANGE-bound periods win rate ~68% avg trade +0.9% expectancy positive Same strategy applied to DOWNTRENDING periods win rate ~38% avg trade −1.7% expectancy strongly negative Applied BLINDLY across all periods win rate ~52% avg trade −0.2% expectancy break-even to negative after costs (synthetic, illustrative of the well-documented pattern)

Read the table carefully. The same rule is genuinely profitable in ranges and genuinely destructive in trends. Blended together they roughly cancel — which is why headlines claiming "RSI doesn't work" and gurus claiming "RSI is magic" are both wrong. The tool's reliability is conditional on regime, exactly as Lesson 8.6 predicted.

This chart is the negative half of the backtest made visible. In ATLAS's downtrend, every RSI-30 "buy" is followed by another leg down. A trader mechanically buying oversold here accumulates loss after loss while the 50-SMA slopes down overhead. No amount of position sizing rescues a rule with negative expectancy in the regime you're trading.

What turns a coin-flip into an edge

Oscillators become reliable only when you stack context and confirmation on top of them:

  1. Regime filter first. Only fade extremes in confirmed ranges; only trade with-trend pullbacks in confirmed trends. This single filter flips the expectancy from negative to positive.
  2. Confluence. An RSI-30 buy is far stronger when it coincides with a horizontal support level, a prior demand zone, or a Fibonacci retracement — multiple independent reasons pointing the same way.
  3. A trigger, not just a condition. Wait for price to confirm: a reversal candle, a break of a micro down-trendline, or a %K/%D cross. The oscillator flags where to look; price action says when to act.
  4. Divergence for exits and warnings. Use divergence to tighten stops or take profits on existing positions rather than as a standalone reversal entry.
Insight.
Think of an oscillator as a filter, not a signal generator. It narrows the field of candidate trades to the moments of stretch; your job is to supply the regime context and the price-action trigger that convert stretch into a decision. Filter + context + trigger = edge. Filter alone = noise.

Realistic expectations

ClaimReality
"RSI 70/30 prints reversals"Only in ranges; in trends it flags strength
"Divergence calls the top"Warns, but can repeat; needs a structure break
"Stochastic crossovers are entries"Reliable in ranges, whipsaw-prone in trends
"Use one oscillator, follow it"Context and confirmation matter more than the tool
Pitfall.
Beware over-optimised backtests that tune RSI thresholds to perfection on past data. An edge that only appears at exactly RSI 27.3 with a 9.2-period lookback is curve-fit noise, not a robust signal. Real oscillator edges are coarse, regime-dependent, and survive small parameter changes.

The mature trader's stance: oscillators are genuinely useful instruments — tachometers that reveal momentum and stretch — but they are not strategies. Their reliability is borrowed from the context you place around them. Respect the regime, demand confirmation, size for the losses that will still come, and momentum oscillators earn a permanent place in your toolkit. Use them blindly and they will, on balance, cost you money.

▸Try It

Goal: prove the regime-dependence to yourself.

  1. Pick one instrument with a year of data. Mark each stretch as range, uptrend, or downtrend.
  2. Log every RSI-below-30 instance and whether buying it (exit at RSI 50) would have won or lost.
  3. Tally results separately by regime — you should see clear wins in ranges and clear losses in downtrends.
  4. Now re-run only the range instances with an added rule: require a support level AND a reversal candle. Note how expectancy improves.
